metadata.title: Research Academic Pathways with Claude: Build the Degree Map Before Drifting

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The conflicting-values case fascinates me, because instead of forcing a single major, it exposed the tension between financial stability and artistic freedom directly. I want to feed it my own clash: scientific curiosity versus a strong preference for never doing math-heavy work. Help me: (1) map that pair to three honest pathways, (2) name for each which value is being made primary and which is being routed outside the career, (3) cite one satisfaction-research finding, like Dik and Duffy, on what happens when a core value is subordinated.

props.command: The conflicting-values case fascinates me, because instead of forcing a single major, it exposed the tension between financial stability and artistic freedom directly. I want to feed it my own clash: scientific curiosity versus a strong preference for never doing math-heavy work. Help me: (1) map that pair to three honest pathways, (2) name for each which value is being made primary and which is being routed outside the career, (3) cite one satisfaction-research finding, like Dik and Duffy, on what happens when a core value is subordinated.
props.segment: Research Academic Pathways with Claude: Build

beats.narration_text: Most students drift through their degree — course by course, semester by semester, with no map of what leads where. Values before plans.

Credential-chasing — choosing a major for its median salary — and values-based planning produce different outcomes in the career satisfaction research. The pathway template does not replace advising — it produces values clarification before degree selection, which is the prerequisite for any advising conversation to be productive.
Ask Claude to build the values-to-pathway mapping template with research citation and 5 campus resources.

props.command: claude "Help me build an academic pathway planning template. Provide: (1) one study showing values-aligned career planning predicts better satisfaction than credential-chasing; (2) a values-to-pathway mapping template — input 5 values, output 3 possible degree areas and 2 specific course sequences per area; (3) a list of 5 campus resources for pathway planning with one-sentence descriptions. My values: helping people, scientific discovery, financial stability, creative work, teaching."

beats.narration_text: The values-to-pathway output: 3 degree areas — Biomedical Sciences, Science Education, Health Informatics. 2 course sequences per area. 5 campus resources: academic advising, career center, major fair, alumni network, degree audit tool.

props.code:
Values: helping people, scientific discovery,
        financial stability, creative work, teaching

Degree Area 1: Biomedical Sciences
  Seq A: Bio101 → Chem101 → Cell Bio → Lab Methods
  Seq B: Bio101 → Neurosci Intro → Research Methods → thesis

Degree Area 2: Science Education
  Seq A: Bio101 → Education Foundations → Methods → student teaching
  Seq B: Chem101 → Curriculum Design → Assessment → capstone

Degree Area 3: Health Informatics
  Seq A: CS Fundamentals → Database Design → Health Data Systems → internship
  Seq B: Statistics → Public Health → Health Informatics → cert track

Research: Dik & Duffy 2009 — values-aligned planning → satisfaction

5 Campus Resources:
  1. Academic Advising — maps requirements to goals
  2. Career Center — connects values to job tracks
  3. Major Fair — live faculty contact per program
  4. Alumni Network — outcomes data from graduates
  5. Degree Audit Tool — tracks requirements in real time

beats.narration_text: Mapping output: Biomedical Sciences — scientific discovery plus helping people — Sequence A: Bio 101 through Lab Methods. Health Informatics — financial stability plus helping people plus scientific discovery — Sequence A: CS fundamentals through internship, Sequence B: statistics through certification track. Each sequence is specific to the input values. Dik and Duffy 2009 cited for values-alignment research.
Run the mapping on conflicting values: financial stability plus artistic freedom.

props.command: claude "Apply the values-to-pathway mapping to conflicting values: financial stability AND artistic freedom. Map the tension honestly — don't resolve it artificially. Show at least 3 pathway options that respect both values to different degrees, and name which value is primary in each option."

beats.narration_text: Conflicting values mapping: Claude correctly identifies the tension — financial stability and artistic freedom rarely optimize together in the same career track. Three options: degree in stable field plus art practice outside work — financial stability primary. Creative field with high commercial demand — graphic design, UX, architecture, median salaries comparable to some STEM fields. Arts administration or cultural nonprofit — artistic environment, lower income. The conflict is not resolved — it is mapped, and the student must decide which value is primary. The template is a values-clarification tool, not a decision maker.

The values-to-pathway template does not produce the degree — it produces the conversation with the advisor that is worth having. Without values clarification, the advising conversation starts from what are my options. With it, it starts from given that I'm optimizing for X, which of these sequences serves it better. The research on career satisfaction is consistent: values alignment predicts satisfaction; credential accumulation does not.
That's the College Success series — ten tools for navigating the institution on your terms.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research Academic Pathways with Claude: Build the Degree Map Before Dr] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
